event: Archaeological evidence indicates early human habitation in the region, with stone tools attesting to long-standing hunter-gatherer communities.
Iron-working technology spreads into the region, enabling more advanced agriculture and tool-making among settled communities.
Bantu-related and West Atlantic-speaking peoples establish farming and fishing settlements along the coast and forested interior.
The ancestors of the Bullom, Temne, and Limba peoples settle the coastal and northern regions, forming small-scale chiefdoms.
Mande-speaking migrants, including Mende and other groups, move into the interior, partly influenced by the decline of the Mali Empire's frontier.
Portuguese explorer Pedro de Sintra charts the coast and names the mountainous peninsula 'Serra Lyoa' (Lion Mountains).
The Portuguese build a trading fort near present-day Freetown, beginning sustained European commerce on the coast.
English captain John Hawkins conducts early slaving voyages along the Sierra Leone coast, foreshadowing the transatlantic slave trade.
The first recorded enslaved Africans shipped to North America from the Sierra Leone region depart, deepening the area's role in the slave trade.
The Atlantic slave trade peaks along the coast, with Bunce Island becoming a major British slave-trading depot.
British abolitionists found the 'Province of Freedom' at Granville Town, settling formerly enslaved black Londoners as a colony for freed people.
The Sierra Leone Company is established to administer the settlement and promote legitimate commerce and Christianity.
Freetown is founded by the Sierra Leone Company, with the arrival of Black Loyalists (Nova Scotian Settlers) from North America.
Jamaican Maroons arrive in Freetown, adding to the colony's population of free black settlers.
Sierra Leone becomes a British Crown Colony, and Freetown is established as a base for the Royal Navy's anti-slavery squadron.
Fourah Bay College is founded in Freetown, becoming the first Western-style university in West Africa.
Britain abolishes slavery throughout its empire, reinforcing Freetown's role as a haven for 'Recaptives' liberated from slave ships.
Britain declares a protectorate over the interior, extending colonial control beyond the coastal colony.
The Hut Tax War erupts as Temne and Mende peoples, led by figures like Bai Bureh, resist British taxation and rule.
A new constitution introduces limited African representation in the colony's Legislative Council.
A new constitution paves the way for decolonization, and the Sierra Leone People's Party (SLPP) emerges as a leading political force.
Sir Milton Margai is appointed Chief Minister as Sierra Leone gains greater internal self-government.
Sierra Leone gains independence from Britain on 27 April, with Milton Margai as its first Prime Minister.
Sierra Leone is admitted as a member state of the United Nations.
Prime Minister Milton Margai dies and is succeeded by his half-brother, Albert Margai.
A disputed election and successive military coups destabilize the country, eventually bringing Siaka Stevens to power.
Sierra Leone becomes a republic, with Siaka Stevens as its first executive President.
A new constitution makes the All People's Congress (APC) the sole legal party, establishing a one-party state.
Joseph Saidu Momoh succeeds Siaka Stevens as President amid economic decline and growing discontent.
The Revolutionary United Front (RUF), backed by Charles Taylor of Liberia, launches a rebellion, igniting an 11-year civil war.
A military coup led by Captain Valentine Strasser overthrows President Momoh, installing the National Provisional Ruling Council.
Ahmad Tejan Kabbah is elected President in multiparty elections, marking a return to civilian rule.
A military coup by the Armed Forces Revolutionary Council ousts Kabbah, who flees into exile.
ECOWAS forces (ECOMOG) restore President Kabbah to power, but fighting with the RUF continues.
The Lome Peace Accord is signed between the government and the RUF, granting rebels concessions in a bid to end the war.
UN peacekeepers (UNAMSIL) and British military intervention help halt RUF advances and stabilize Freetown.
The civil war is officially declared over, having killed tens of thousands and displaced millions; Kabbah is re-elected President.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone is established to prosecute those most responsible for war crimes.
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission publishes its report documenting atrocities committed during the civil war.
Ernest Bai Koroma of the APC wins the presidential election, marking a peaceful democratic transfer of power.
Former Liberian president Charles Taylor is convicted by the Special Court for aiding war crimes in Sierra Leone.
A devastating Ebola virus epidemic strikes Sierra Leone, killing nearly 4,000 people and crippling the economy.
A catastrophic mudslide near Freetown kills over 1,000 people after heavy rains and deforestation.
Julius Maada Bio of the SLPP is elected President, defeating the ruling APC candidate.
President Julius Maada Bio is re-elected in a contested vote, and an attempted armed uprising is suppressed later that year.
